comment_text.sn4.2:1.3: At SN 41.4:3.3 such a rain is regarded as pleasantly cooling.

root_text.sn4.2:1.2: ekaṁ samayaṁ bhagavā uruvelāyaṁ viharati najjā nerañjarāya tīre ajapālanigrodhamūle paṭhamābhisambuddho.
root_text.sn4.2:1.3: Tena kho pana samayena bhagavā rattandhakāratimisāyaṁ abbhokāse nisinno hoti, devo ca ekamekaṁ phusāyati.
root_text.sn4.2:1.4: Atha kho māro pāpimā bhagavato bhayaṁ chambhitattaṁ lomahaṁsaṁ uppādetukāmo mahantaṁ hatthirājavaṇṇaṁ abhinimminitvā yena bhagavā tenupasaṅkami.
root_text.sn4.2:1.11: Atha kho bhagavā “māro ayaṁ pāpimā” iti viditvā māraṁ pāpimantaṁ gāthāya ajjhabhāsi:
root_text.sn4.2:3.1: Atha kho māro pāpimā “jānāti maṁ bhagavā, jānāti maṁ sugato”ti dukkhī dummano tatthevantaradhāyīti.

translation_text.sn4.2:0.3: In the Form of an Elephant King
translation_text.sn4.2:1.2: At one time, when he was first awakened, the Buddha was staying in Uruvelā at the root of the goatherd’s banyan tree on the bank of the Nerañjarā River.
translation_text.sn4.2:1.3: Now at that time the Buddha was meditating in the open during the dark of night, while a gentle rain drizzled down.
translation_text.sn4.2:1.4: Then Māra the Wicked, wanting to make the Buddha feel fear, terror, and goosebumps, manifested in the form of a huge elephant king and approached him.
translation_text.sn4.2:1.5: Its head was like a huge block of soapstone.
translation_text.sn4.2:1.7: Its tusks were like pure silver.
translation_text.sn4.2:1.9: Its trunk was like a long plough pole.
translation_text.sn4.2:1.11: Then the Buddha, knowing that this was Māra the Wicked, addressed him in verse:
translation_text.sn4.2:2.2: you’ve made forms beautiful and ugly.
translation_text.sn4.2:3.1: Then Māra the Wicked, thinking, “The Buddha knows me! The Holy One knows me!” miserable and sad, vanished right there.

reference_text.sn4.2:1.1: bj13.190, csp1ed12.105, csp2ed12.105, ms12S1_776, msdiv138, ndp12.104, pts-vp-pli1ed1.104, pts-vp-pli2ed1.233
